network.overall_strategy: This network is a literature-constrained scenario map for prebiotic arginine formation rather than a claim of solved end-to-end synthesis. It centers the best-supported constructive branch: the Patel-Tsanakopoulou-Sutherland cyanosulfidic route from HCN/acetylene to an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor under anaerobic UV photoredox conditions, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. It also includes the historically important direct ornithine + cyanamide guanylation concept, explicitly marked as incomplete because ornithine provenance from simple feedstocks is unresolved. A speculative prebiotic urea-cycle analog through citrulline is included only as a low-confidence branch, and the trace supercritical CO2/H2O report is retained as a marginal side route. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively to provision robust feedstocks such as formate and pyruvate and to illustrate Hydrothermal ↔ Surface interconnection, while arginine-forming chemistry is assigned primarily to surface cyanosulfidic and later biochemical concentration/preservation contexts. Ten distinct pathways are enumerated by recombining the supported branch points, convergence nodes, and post-formation preservation/utilization steps without overstating unsupported chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, H2-rich, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases generates surface-bound formyl equivalents released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Used as a conservative hydrothermal feedstock-provisioning step consistent with iron-sulfur vent chemistry and CO2 reduction on greigite/mackinawite analogs; this supports broader prebiotic carbon fixation framing but is not claimed as a direct arginine-forming step (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Jordan et al., 2021; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate provisioning from reduced C1 carbon

conditions.other: subseafloor or vent-mixing setting; speculative network-connecting step

reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and C-C bond formation from reduced carbon species furnish alpha-keto acid inventory including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Included only as a cautious hydrothermal alpha-keto-acid provisioning node because pyruvate is central to prebiotic carbon metabolism, but direct prebiotic pyruvate synthesis under these exact conditions is less firmly established than formate production; therefore this reaction is explicitly speculative and used for network connectivity rather than as a validated arginine route (Barge et al., 2019; Kitadai, 2015; Amend & Shock, 1998).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric chemistry

conditions.other: strong UV, anoxic reduced-gas photochemistry context; schematic environmental source step

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reduced nitrogen-carbon photochemistry furnishes cyanamide in shallow surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies cyanamide as prebiotically credible from UV irradiation of reduced CH4/NH3/H2O mixtures and as a central node in cyanosulfidic chemistry. This node is included as an environmental source representation rather than a stoichiometrically complete laboratory reaction; it avoids unsupported formamide compression criticized in prior attempts (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015; Powner, Gerland & Sutherland, 2009).

reactions.name: Surface UV production of guanidine from ammonium cyanide chemistry

conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5, 254 nm UV, 7 days to 10 weeks, ammonium cyanide solution, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oligomerization and rearrangement of cyanide/ammonium species gives guanidine as a stable nitrogen-rich product.
reactions.reasoning: Directly follows Lohrmann's demonstration that UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide gives guanidine in 8-20% yield, while emphasizing that guanidine availability does not solve selective arginine formation on a C5 backbone (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox in phosphate buffer on mineral-rich surface
conditions.other: 35°C, UV irradiation, H2S present, anaerobic cyanosulfidic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Cu photoredox-promoted radical/cross-coupling chemistry between HCN-derived carbon and acetylene forms acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key entry to the Sutherland arginine-precursor branch and is taken directly from the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network under anaerobic UV, Cu, H2S, and phosphate-buffered conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017). Mineral agents are included as required environmental supports, while the literature-defined Cu photoredox system is specified in conditions.

reactions.name: Addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition of ammonia to the activated alkene nitrile yields beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly part of the Patel/Tsanakopoulou arginine-precursor branch from acrylonitrile to beta-aminopropionitrile under mild cyanosulfidic conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with H2S and UV

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile oxidation state furnishes the corresponding aldehyde, beta-aminopropionaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies reduction of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde as a step in the Patel route; this node therefore follows that literature-guided sequence without adding alternative chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide and onward cyanosulfidic assembly to arginine aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox network with phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 35°C, UV irradiation, H2S present, anaerobic; reaction compressed to the literature-described guanylation plus subsequent photoreduction/HCN addition sequence producing compound 46 analog

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attack on cyanamide installs a guanidino functionality on the C3 aldehyde intermediate; subsequent photoreduction and HCN incorporation in the cyanosulfidic network furnish the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that the especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide suggests production of compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, alongside proline precursor chemistry. Because the late-stage sequence is described but not fully separated with quantitative yields, it is represented here as a compressed literature-faithful node rather than invented substeps (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools or during downstream processing

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile precursor into the alpha-amino acid arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is standard in Strecker-related and cyanosulfidic networks; here it is the literature-designated completion of the Sutherland arginine precursor route, though no full continuous one-pot demonstration to arginine has been achieved (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration with alkaline aqueous conditions
conditions.other: conceptual prebiotic analog of aqueous guanylation; classical reaction known with base and modern high-yield aqueous catalysis at 100°C but not demonstrated continuously under strict prebiotic conditions

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group of ornithine attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide, directly forming the guanidinium side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central two-phase concept highlighted in the report: build the ornithine backbone, then install the guanidinium group with cyanamide. The chemistry is historically validated by Schulze and Winterstein and modernly by aqueous cyanamide guanylation, but a fully prebiotic continuous demonstration remains absent; this reaction is therefore literature-supported yet incomplete at the systems level (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Environmental occurrence and concentration of ornithine

conditions.other: placeholder inventory node representing ornithine detection in spark discharge and tentative meteoritic delivery rather than a validated single-step synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Environmental supply node representing accumulation of ornithine from broader prebiotic chemistry and/or exogenous input rather than a mechanistically solved transformation.
reactions.reasoning: This node is intentionally labeled as an inventory/occurrence representation because the report states ornithine is detected in volcanic spark discharge experiments and tentatively in Murchison, but no robust simple-feedstock route is established here. It is included to close the historically important ornithine + cyanamide branch without falsely inventing unsupported ornithine synthesis chemistry (Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Meierhenrich et al., 2004).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate generation of cyanate and citrulline inventory

conditions.catalyst: UV-assisted HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis on mineral-rich surface
conditions.other: anoxic shallow-pool HCN oligomer chemistry; intentionally represented as an inventory node

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis yields a complex product distribution that can include cyanate-bearing species and reported citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: Prior critiques noted overdefined stoichiometry. This reaction is therefore explicitly represented as an inventory-generating hydrolysate node, not a precise single stoichiometric conversion. The report supports citrulline identification in HCN oligomer hydrolysates and cyanate as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl surrogate (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Speculative carbamoylation branch linking ornithine and cyanate to citrulline pool
reactions.mechanism: The terminal amine of ornithine is carbamoylated by cyanate to furnish citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly discusses ornithine to citrulline via cyanate as a plausible prebiotic analog to biological carbamoyl phosphate chemistry, but notes the overall pathway remains hypothetical. This node is therefore retained as a low-confidence literature-inspired branch only (Juarez et al., 2021; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Speculative citrulline-to-arginine conversion via cyanamide

conditions.other: explicitly hypothetical post-surface conversion in concentrated prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Hypothetical activation/guanylation-related conversion of citrulline-derived functionality into arginine without the biological argininosuccinate sequence.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is explicitly speculative and included only because the report identifies a hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog as a discussed route. No direct experimental prebiotic demonstration exists, so this step should be interpreted as a low-confidence connector rather than established chemistry (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Cunin et al., 1986).

reactions.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O arginine formation from pyruvate and hydroxylamine

conditions.catalyst: supercritical CO2/H2O medium; mineral surfaces included as environmental supports
conditions.other: 60°C, supercritical CO2/H2O, product detected after acid hydrolysis; very low yield

reactions.mechanism: Unresolved multistep condensation/rearrangement chemistry in compressed CO2/H2O medium gave trace arginine among hydrolysis products.
reactions.reasoning: Included exactly as a marginal literature side branch because Fujioka et al. reported only trace arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O. The mechanism was not elucidated and this route conflicts with broader evidence that arginine is thermolabile, so it is intentionally down-weighted (Fujioka et al., 2009; Körner et al., 2021; Miller & Bada, 1988).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite adsorption and preservation of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7, clay-rich mineral interface; concentration/protection rather than net synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Cation exchange inserts guanidinium-bearing arginine into clay interlayers, concentrating and shielding it from decomposition.
reactions.reasoning: Retained as a biochemical preservation node because the report emphasizes that montmorillonite selectively adsorbs cationic amino acids such as arginine 30-50× more strongly than zwitterionic amino acids, potentially protecting any arginine once formed. This is not counted as constructive synthesis but is crucial to realistic network persistence (Catalano et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide bond formation from arginine to arginyl-arginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay/mineral interfaces; concentration-enhanced dehydration

reactions.mechanism: Repeated dehydration-rehydration drives condensation of arginine into short peptides once the amino acid is concentrated on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: This is a downstream utilization step consistent with broader prebiotic peptide condensation and with observations that arginine interlayer complexes on montmorillonite can undergo peptide bond formation under dry high-temperature conditions. It is included to connect target synthesis to biochemical assembly without pretending it validates upstream arginine origin (Campbell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate-linked hydroxylamine inventory

conditions.other: speculative inventory-support step connecting hydrothermal reduced-nitrogen chemistry to the Fujioka branch

reactions.mechanism: Reduced iron-rich settings can sustain reactive nitrogen species including hydroxylamine-like intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is explicitly speculative and included only to give the trace Fujioka branch a connected precursor context. The report states hydroxylamine is itself an advanced intermediate rather than a primitive feedstock, so this node should be interpreted as weak support rather than established chemistry (Fujioka et al., 2009; Kitadai, 2015).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine precursor route
pathways.description: Best-supported literature pathway from HCN and acetylene through acrylonitrile, beta-aminopropionitrile, beta-aminopropionaldehyde, cyanamide-enabled guanylation, and hydrolysis to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with hydrothermal carbon provisioning backdrop
pathways.description: Same constructive surface route as P1, but explicitly linked to hydrothermal reduced-carbon provisioning via formate and pyruvate pools feeding broader surface protometabolism.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation route
pathways.description: Historically established concept in which pre-existing ornithine reacts with cyanamide to produce arginine directly; chemically plausible but incomplete because ornithine origin is unresolved.

pathways.name: Ornithine guanylation with post-formation clay preservation
pathways.description: Direct ornithine plus cyanamide guanylation followed by montmorillonite concentration/protection of arginine.

pathways.name: Ornithine guanylation followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed by direct guanylation is subsequently incorporated into a short peptide under wet-dry biochemical cycling.

pathways.name: Speculative prebiotic urea-cycle analog from HCN hydrolysate citrulline inventory
pathways.description: Low-confidence route in which HCN oligomer hydrolysates furnish citrulline and/or cyanate-bearing inventory that is converted to arginine using cyanamide.

pathways.name: Speculative ornithine-to-citrulline-to-arginine route
pathways.description: Hypothetical prebiotic analog of the biological urea cycle: ornithine carbamoylation by cyanate to citrulline followed by speculative conversion to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic precursor route with clay preservation
pathways.description: The Patel-Tsanakopoulou branch completed to arginine and then stabilized by montmorillonite adsorption in a biochemical setting.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic precursor route extended into peptide chemistry
pathways.description: Arginine arising from the best-supported aminonitrile branch is subsequently incorporated into a short oligopeptide under wet-dry cycling.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O branch
pathways.description: Marginal route based on the only reported abiotic arginine detection from pyruvate and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions; included strictly as a low-confidence literature edge case.
